name: Finite-difference truncation error and order of accuracy
principles: Taylor expansion of the discretised function
leading-order error scaling as a power of grid spacing
consistency as a prerequisite for convergence
conventions: f is sufficiently smooth — at least C^3 for the centred 2nd-order error estimate above
the O(h^2) term means the leading truncation error is proportional to h^2 (centred difference is 2nd-order accurate)
forward / backward 1st-order: f'(x) ~ (f(x+h) - f(x))/h + O(h); cheaper but less accurate
higher-order stencils: 4th-order centred uses 5 points: f'(x) ~ (-f(x-2h) + 8 f(x-h) - 8 f(x+h) + f(x+2h))/(12 h) + O(h^4)
second-derivative centred: f''(x) ~ (f(x+h) - 2 f(x) + f(x-h))/h^2 + O(h^2)
expectedLimits: h -> 0: truncation error -> 0 algebraically; method is consistent with the differential operator
convergence rate: error decays as h^p where p is the formal order of accuracy (verifiable by halving h and observing factor-of-2^p error reduction)
Lax equivalence theorem: consistency + stability => convergence (for well-posed linear problems)
non-smooth f (kinks, discontinuities): Taylor expansion fails near the singularity; effective order drops to first or below at the kink
round-off-limited h: at very small h, subtraction of nearly-equal values amplifies machine precision noise as 1/h; optimal h ~ sqrt(epsilon_machine) for first derivatives
non-uniform grids: stencil coefficients change; care needed to maintain order of accuracy at interior nodes
references: Strikwerda — Finite Difference Schemes and Partial Differential Equations, ch.1-3
LeVeque — Finite Difference Methods for ODEs and PDEs, ch.1
Morton & Mayers — Numerical Solution of Partial Differential Equations, ch.2
Gustafsson, Kreiss & Oliger — Time Dependent Problems and Difference Methods, ch.4
